MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Of Issues; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Apr-01-2022 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE CALIFORNIA STATE'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Of Issues.

Defendant Board of Trustees of the California State University's ("CSU") motion for summary judgment is granted.

The Court finds all of Plaintiff's causes of action to be time-barred. Plaintiff filed an administrative complaint with the Department of Fair Employment and Housing ("DFEH") on August 2, 2017 and DFEH issued a right-to-sue notice the same day. (Herrera Decl., Ex. B.) California Government Code section 12965 requires employees to file a civil suit within one year of a right-to-sue notice. Plaintiff filed suit in federal court exactly one year later, on August 2, 2018. (Id. at Ex. C.) The state-law claims asserted against CSU in that suit were dismissed based on Eleventh Amendment sovereign immunity. (Id. at Ex. E.) As such, Plaintiff's argument that 28 U.S.C. section 1367(d) tolls the one-year statute of limitations fails. (Raygor v. Regents of Univ. of Minnesota (2002) 534 U.S. 533, 548 [Section 1367(d) "does not toll the period of limitations for state law claims asserted against nonconsenting state defendants that are dismissed on Eleventh Amendment grounds."].)

Furthermore, even if section 1367(d) did toll the statute of limitations, Plaintiff would have been required to file this action by April 14, 2019, 30 days after the federal court dismissed her state-law claims. (Herrera Decl., Ex. G.) Plaintiff did not file this action until May 13, 2019. (Id. at Ex. H.)

Plaintiff appears to argue that her suit was timely because she filed a government claim for damages on November 26, 2018. (Opp., 15:14-16.) This argument misconstrues the Government Claims Act, which requires a plaintiff to timely file a claim for damages as a condition precedent to filing a lawsuit against a public entity. (Cal. Govt Code sec. 911.2.) In any case, compliance with the Government Claims Act does not negate Plaintiff's obligation to comply with the statute of limitations under Government Code section 12965.

Accordingly, Plaintiff's causes of action are time-barred and the Court need not reach the arguments on the merits.

order is signed.

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

Defendant The Board Of Trustees Of The California State University'S Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Nov-13-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant The Board Of Trustees Of The California State University'S Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint:

Defendant Board of Trustees of the California State University demurs to three of the numbered counts in plaintiff Rabab Abdulhadi's first amended complaint (FAC).
1: Breach of Contract. Defendant's lead demurrer is that "Plaintiff's employment is governed by statute, not by contract." Thus, civil service employees like plaintiff "cannot state a cause of action for breach of contract." (See Kim v. Regents of University of California (2000) 80 Cal.Ap.4th 160, 164.) Plaintiff's opposition does not address this demurrer, so it is sustained with leave to amend.
2&3: Govt. Code 12490 (employment discrimination) and 12940(k) (failure to prevent discrimination). The demurrers to these two counts are overruled.
The parties agree that Yanowitz v. L'Oreal USA, Inc. (2005) 36 Cal.4th 1028 is a controlling case on adverse employment actions. In it, the California Supreme Court held that "adverse treatment that is reasonably likely to impair a reasonable employee's job performance or prospects for advancement or performance" suffices. (Id. at 1054-55.) Taken as true - as they must be on demurrer - the FAC's allegations of "[m]onths of unwarranted and public criticism," "implied threat of termination," supervisors "actively solicit negative information about her," criticism "in written memoranda," unwarranted travel audits, purposeful administrative delays and exclusion from task forces meet the Yanowitz standard.
As to discriminatory motive, because defendants rarely admit it, motive must usually be inferred. The FAC alleges anti-Palestinian slurs in the university president's presence, the president's own derogatory attitude and the disparate treatment of Palestinian, Arab and Muslim faculty and students - all of which must be accepted as true on a demurrer. This suffices.

order signed.
